data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Foxconn Profit Jumps, But Tariffs Cast A Shadow
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Foxconn Q1 profit soars on AI demand; tariffs woes weigh on FY outlook. (00:28) Airbnb goes beyond rentals with full-service travel offerings. (02:34) YouTube bags rights to stream NFL game for the first time. (03:22)

This is an abridged transcript.

Foxconn Technology's (OTCPK:FXCOF) first quarter profit jumped 91% beating estimates driven by demand for AI servers, but the outlook was overshadowed by uncertainty around tariffs.

The Apple (AAPL) supplier — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — said net profit attributable to the parent company surged 91% year-over -year to NT$42.12B ($1.39B).

The numbers beat analysts' estimate of NT$37.8B, according to a report from Reuters.

Foxconn's Chairman Young Liu said on an earnings call that U.S. tariffs will bring more challenges and his outlook for the full year was more cautious than before, the report added.

The company has a big manufacturing presence in China, though the U.S. and China have agreed to a significant reduction in tariffs on each other’s goods for 90 days.

Foxconn adjusted the 2025 outlook to significant growth from strong growth citing foreign exchange.

Liu, in the earnings release, pointed out that in addition to tariffs, geopolitics, and changes in monetary policy around the world may affect the global economy, and even though current sales forecasts have not changed much, the exchange rate may affect revenue after conversion into New Taiwan dollars. However, revenue in U.S. dollars remains unchanged.

Foxconn's first quarter revenue climbed 24% year-over -year to NT$1.644T.

The company said the keyword to sum up first quarter performance is without a doubt: “A.I.” Liu reiterated the view that 2025 is the first year of AI, saying that the performance of the AI server business is quite impressive, with revenue growing by more than 50% compared to the same period last year, indicating that demand for computing power is still very strong.

Foxconn noted that AI server revenue in the second quarter is expected to nearly double both on-quarter and on-year basis.

Nvidia (NVDA) is among the company's prominent customers.

Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) is upgrading its offerings just in time for summer travel.

The company debuted Airbnb Services and Experiences.

Guests can find professionals that offer services ranging from spa treatments and photography to local tour guides and cultural experiences.

Airbnb says they thoroughly vet these professionals and they are required to have an average of 10 years of experience. In addition, there’s an identity qualification process and they have to submit relevant licenses and certifications.

The offerings will be available beginning this summer on the newly designed Airbnb app.

Airbnb (ABNB) shares ended Tuesday’s regular trading session with a gain of nearly 3%, putting the stock in the plus column for a fifth straight day.

YouTube (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is getting into the football business.

The company picked up exclusive rights to stream its first NFL game.

YouTube will stream the Friday night Week 1 game of the 2025 NFL season. The game will be available to stream for free on YouTube globally, except in Canada and certain other countries.

The financial terms for the streaming agreement were not disclosed.

YouTube's business chief Mary Ellen Coe said this will be an “interactive viewing experience” with “creators right at the center of the experience."

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Tesla may get new pay package for CEO Elon Musk - report

EU said to weigh higher tariffs on Ukrainian imports to protect its farmers

Nvidia CEO pay jumps to nearly $50M amid AI-driven growth, net worth swells

Catalyst watch:

  • Tyson Foods (TSN) CEO Donnie King will participate in a fireside chat at the 20th Annual BMO Global Farm to Market Conference.

  • Upstart (UPST) will hold its AI Day event.

  • The three-day Consensus 2025 event will take place in Toronto. The three-day crypto conference hosted by CoinDesk is one of the most highly anticipated events of the year.

  • The 2025 Sohn Investment Conference will be held in New York City. The conference has led to share price jumps for some stocks in the past after hedge funds made pitches.

  • The two-day Twilio (TWLO) Signal conference will begin in San Francisco. The tech company is expected to showcase its latest products and features.

  • Netflix (NFLX) and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) will hold presentations in New York City as part of Upfront Week. Alphabet's (GOOG) YouTube will also hold its Brandcast event.

  • Harley-Davidson (HOG) will hold its annual shareholder meeting amid activist pressure from H Partners. The outcome of the board vote will shape Harley-Davidson's leadership and strategic direction.

  • Notable companies due to appear at the two-day J.P. Morgan Global Technology, Media and Communications Conference include The New York Times (NYT), Wayfair (W), Carvana (CVNA), IBM (IBM), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), and Charter Communications (CHTR).

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are flat. Crude oil is down 1.2% at $62/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.7% at $103,000. Gold is down 0.6% at $3,233.

The FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is down 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) -15% — Shares slumped after the company pulled its full-year forecast, citing macro uncertainty, and said it is “actively evaluating” its forward plans.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 9:10 am Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson will speak on the economic outlook before the virtual Annual Conference of Second District Directors and Advisors.

  • 5:40 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in fireside chat before the California Bankers Association 25th Annual Conference and Directors Forum.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs Calm Down, PayPal Heats Up - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) is pretty much everywhere these days; whenever you're checking out online, there always seems to be that PayPal option. What you might not know is that every time you choose that PayPal option, they swipe about 3% from that transaction. Now, the average transaction through their platform is only about $64, so most shoppers don't notice that small transaction fee, but in reality, all that money adds up. So, now we’ve established their revenue is heavily dependent on the volume of transactions, what are some of the drivers that, I think, will boost platform engagement in 2025? Well, there's actually quite a lot. PayPal doesn't offer a physical product, so user satisfaction all comes down to the customer experience; they master this, and I'm expecting more people will be using their platform on that checkout page with the revamped customer experience this year.

Of course, like with all companies, AI integration will take place, but I also see a rebranded checkout experience, which makes "buy now and pay later" for items even more streamlined. PayPal stock is down 16% year-to-date, currently trading at $72, which makes it a bargain, in my opinion. The stock is also gaining more momentum, up 25% since its high $50s low in early April. I believe the stock will easily break into the $80s range.

So, How Does PayPal Make Money, Exactly?

Well, PayPal charges quite small fees on the back of transactions, and while these transactions are quite small, the fees add up. 436 million PayPal accounts are active worldwide, up 2% from this time last year. The average account carried out roughly 60 transactions on PayPal each month, a number they hope to improve in 2025. The core of their revenue, of course, comes from transactions, the 3% they charge; this revenue figure is dependent on consumer spending, total transactions go up, and PayPal can collect a bigger cut. Another revenue area for PayPal is its value-added services, which include things like interest collected on payments and loans, as well as subscriptions and referral fees. About 90% of revenue is generated from their transactional fees, which is why management tries so hard to boost the total transactions figure. PayPal posted strong 1Q25 earnings back at the start of April. The company generated around $7.79 billion in revenue in a quarter that saw a whopping $417 billion in transactions in 1Q. This revenue was in line with the $7.85 billion expected by Wall Street. Earnings per share beat consensus with $1.33 reported against $1.16 expected. What investors really liked is that they guided higher for 2Q, the company said transaction margin dollars should increase 4% to 5% to between $3.75 billion and $3.8 billion. I believe PayPal can live up to the hype. If you trace their total payment volume (TPV), you will see a steady upward trend. TPV increases roughly 2% each quarter; it's clear that more and more money passes through PayPal in the long run.

Trump Cuts China Tariffs = Spending Goes Up

Trump finally came to a deal with China this week; tariffs on Chinese imports will stay at 30%, while US exports to China have dropped to just 10%. It's no secret that a large number of US consumer goods purchased online come from China. Since the high tariffs at the start of the year, analysts feared the economy would experience slow growth and high inflation, but now it seems this won't be the case. Consumer spending in March actually surged 0.7% as shoppers feared more incoming tariffs could drive prices higher, and this morning, April inflation data showed inflation is not as bad as expected, up 0.2% versus estimates for 0.3%. This strong online spending trend will continue into 2Q25. While I believe tariffs on China will not come back, many in the US will fear the opposite and continue to spend online to take advantage of current prices. We should see transactional volume continue this same upward trend, despite a small slowdown in 1Q25, as seen on the chart I made above.

The Two Things I Like About PayPal:

1. An improved Look for 2025

As mentioned, PayPal is all about the customer experience. If you can make PayPal easier or even cheaper to use when you're at that checkout page, TPV will go up. On the last earnings call, CEO Alex Chriss stressed the need for a faster and simpler checkout experience; they want the number of clicks a customer goes through on the checkout page to be fewer. To do this, they're aiming to boost their buy now pay later option, after all the ones that buy then pay later spend on average 33% more and complete 17% more transactions. These kinds of numbers make PayPal want to promote that option when users check out. PayPal likes this option for other benefits too, usually a nice amount of buyers' fault on payments, which include late fees, and they also collect slightly higher interest on payments. The good news for investors is that the number of buy now, pay later buyers is growing, and history tells us the US consumer loves choosing this option. In 1Q25, pay later transactions grew more than 20%. PayPal should expect nice tailwinds in 2025 as these numbers hint at an increasing average transaction value per person. Currently, this figure is at $64. Venmo transactions were also up in 1Q25, the company boasted an extra 2 million first-time Venmo users this quarter and roughly 40 million active Venmo debit accounts monthly.

Elsewhere, they aim to up their ad content, recently expanding their ad business into the UK and launching quite a few off-site ads. What the ad market likes about PayPal ads is that they know where the money goes. PayPal databases are full of consumer spending trends. For each business, they can offer the direction of spending on products and personalize ads to get a much higher return on ad spending.

2. Agentic Commerce

Now, it's no secret that AI is slowly making its way into every aspect of life, and online shopping is no exception. PayPal is betting on agentic commerce being the next big thing in the industry. What that means exactly is that more and more people will use AI to complete all aspects of their shopping experience, including the checkout page. For example, we are already seeing this with people replenishing household items by telling Alexa to restock. What PayPal wants to do is integrate its payment method with agentic shopping experiences. That is, AI agents are able to work better through PayPal with fewer payment hoops than regular banking options. To get a head start in this game, PayPal is rolling out an MCP server, an industry-first server which “enables the leading AI agent frameworks to seamlessly integrate with PayPal APIs”. I believe they're right to start accounting for AI agent shopping from this early on, as multiple big shopping platforms are moving into automated ordering. Whether it's Amazon (AMZN) baskets or eBay offers going through when you're sleeping, people will choose PayPal if it's less hassle for them.

Risks

On the last earnings call, management mentioned some macroeconomic uncertainty going into 2025, which could explain them remaining cautious and choosing not to raise their full-year guidance. Their CFI, Jamie Miller, mentioned this on the call:

Tariff-related concerns and news flow have likely resulted in some spend being pulled forward.

What we know about this is that cross-border trade may experience some slowdown if tariffs come back. However, the news surrounding the latest deal between the US and China makes me positive that tariffs won't come back. Elsewhere, strong US dollar headwinds translate into fewer foreign revenues when converting revenue from overseas transactions. We may experience a decline in cross-border transactions.

Valuation

If I were writing this article at the start of the year, PayPal stock would be trading at a lovely $90. That figure is what investors were used to before February 3rd, before PayPal reported disappointing 4Q24 earnings. Since then, they tumbled a whopping 35%, hitting a low of $57, and while the stock isn't that cheap anymore, I still believe it to be undervalued compared to the next 6-month trajectory. PayPal stock has already started that slow recovery, rising slowly since that 52-week low to $72. Forward price to sales is at 2.15, lower than the sector median of 2.75. We can see the same trend across the forward EV to sales, with a ratio of 2.22 against a sector median of 3.06. Over the past month, the stock has really gained some momentum, handling itself better through tariffs than the S&P 500. Currently, it's up 15% over the past month, compared to 9% on the S&P. The market cap sits at roughly $70 billion, with an average share volume of 12 million.

What's Next?

PayPal 2Q25 earnings are a long way off; historically, they see their big boost in earnings during the Q4 season. For Q2, I expect a steady 2% growth in revenue, again, revenue will be fueled by transactional revenue. Investors using PayPal personally should expect a rebranded checkout experience, with more pressure on buyers to purchase and pay later in installments. Investors should keep an eye on consumer spending for May. If we see better-than-expected results, we can assume PayPal will post some nice TPV figures. After all this is said and done, I believe PayPal is still remaining ahead of the game in 2025, people still have every reason to check out with a PayPal option. I initiate this stock with a buy. $72 is a nice entry point as this stock climbs slowly back up to the mid-80s.

data.title: Gator Capital Management Q1 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Gator Financial Partners:

We are pleased to provide you with Gator Financial Partners, LLC’s (the “Fund” or “GFP”) 2025 1st quarter investor letter. This letter reviews the Fund’s 2025 Q1, discusses how we are thinking about tariffs, and shares our investment thesis on Virtus Investment Partners (“Virtus” or “VRTS”).

Review of 2025 Q1 Performance

During the 1st quarter of 2025, the Fund had mixed performance. We outperformed the broader market but lagged the Financials sector benchmark. The large banks and the large insurers had a strong Q1, and we have not owned these companies in several years. We believe both the valuations and the growth rates of the largest financial companies are unattractive. We much prefer the faster growing and less expensive small- and mid-cap financial companies. But, in Q1, the stock market didn’t agree with our view. Our long positions in Societe Generale (OTCPK:SCGLY)(OTCPK:SCGLF), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY), Barclays PLC (BCS)(OTCPK:BCLYF), and Sallie Mae were the top contributors to the Fund’s performance. The largest detractors were long positions in First Citizens Bancshares (FCNCA), PayPal (PYPL), Virtus Investment Partners (VRTS), Global Payments (GPN), and UMB Financial (UMBF).

Tariffs: A Risky Gambit, But Likely to Fade

We view the recent sweeping tariff proposals as an unforced policy error. While improving America’s trade positioning is a legitimate long-term objective, imposing broad-based tariffs on nearly all global imports with limited warning is an unnecessarily reckless approach.

The idea that tariffs will bring back large-scale manufacturing employment to the U.S. is misguided. American factory workers themselves do not aspire for their children or grandchildren to work in factories. Reshoring manufacturing at scale is a political talking point, not an economic reality.

That said, we believe these tariffs will ultimately be short-lived. While we have no special insight into the Trump administration’s strategy, we are relying on the stock market’s interpretation of unfolding events. The sharp sell-off on the morning of April 7th marked a peak in capitulation, in our view. The strong equity market performance over the last three weeks of April suggests investors are pricing in a less extreme path forward and may be signaling that equities have found a near-term bottom.

We continue to believe that public markets, in aggregate, often do a better job forecasting outcomes than any individual analyst or investor—particularly during periods of heightened uncertainty. Market action may be reflecting expectations for a political reversal, potentially influenced by surprising recent electoral wins by liberal parties in Canada and Australia. Some investors could be extrapolating these outcomes to project Democratic gains in the 2026 U.S. midterm elections, which could lead to a reversal of Trump-era tariffs.

As investors, we do not need to predict the precise political pathway to lower tariffs. What matters for our positioning is the judgment call: will these tariffs persist at harmful levels, or will they be rolled back? For now, our base case is that tariffs will be reduced to more sustainable levels. We will continue to challenge this assumption and remain open to new information.

In the meantime, we continue to find compelling investment opportunities within the Financials sector, where we believe valuations already discount a significant amount of macroeconomic uncertainty. One industry that presents a near-term opportunity continues to be Regional Banks. Regional Banks were one of the industries hardest hit by the Trump Liberation Day announcement. We believe top-down macro investors continue to wrongly use shorts against Regional Banks as a way to express their view of increased recession risk. There is a disconnect between the reality of the credit quality in the regional banking system and these investors’ perception of the risk. As we got into bank earnings season during late April, the bank management teams discussed the strong credit quality of the sector and the banks’ stocks price have largely recovered.

Investment Thesis on Virtus Investment Partners

We believe Virtus Investment Partners offers a compelling investment from current levels. Virtus Investment Partners is a multi-boutique traditional investment manager. The company has several investment affiliates with their own investment teams and strategies. Virtus provides centralized sales, middle- and back-office services to support these teams. Virtus has grown by using cash flow to acquire additional investment firms. It keeps the investment teams of the acquisitions in place and centralizes sales and operations. We successfully owned Virtus for the Fund several years ago.

Our investment thesis is below:

  1. High-Quality Business Due to Recurring Revenue and High Free Cash Flow - Virtus Investment Partners boasts a high-quality business model underpinned by recurring revenue streams and elevated free cash flow. The firm's strategic focus on asset management ensures steady income through management fees, which are largely predictable and stable. This recurring revenue is a testament to the company's robust client relationships and consistent performance, providing a strong foundation for continued growth. Furthermore, high free cash flow enables Virtus to reinvest in its business, pursue acquisitions, and return capital to shareholders, enhancing overall value.
  2. Low Valuation on Price to Earnings and EV/EBITDA Basis - VRTS has an attractive valuation. It trades for 6.5x price-to-earnings (P/E) and a shockingly low 3.0x on Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EV/EBITDA) basis. VRTS has a strong balance sheet that matches the strong cash flow profile of the business. The company only has $100 million of net debt, which is about 0.3x EBITDA. In addition, VRTS carries a portfolio of seed investments in start-up funds and products valued at $140M. It recycles this seed capital into incubating new funds and products as existing seed investments payoff or fail. Virtus also has about $140M in CLO equity investments. These investments are made as its advisors form new CLOs. We subtract the seed investments and the CLO equity investments from Enterprise Value when we quote the 3.0x EV/EBITDA multiple. One could argue that seed investments and CLO equity investments are necessary to run the business. If we excluded these two items from Enterprise Value because they are necessary to run the business, we still calculate a very low 4.0x EV/EBITDA multiple. Virtus trades at a lower multiple compared to its peers.
  3. Low Debt Levels Provide Management Flexibility - The firm's prudent approach to debt management further strengthens its investment appeal. Virtus Investment Partners maintains low debt levels, which afford management significant flexibility to act on M&A or share repurchase opportunities. This conservative financial stance mitigates risk and enhances the firm's ability to sustain operations during economic downturns.
  4. Potential for Earnings Accretive Acquisition - Virtus Investment Partners is well-positioned to pursue earnings accretive acquisitions. The firm's strong balance sheet and free cash flow provide the necessary capital to acquire additional investment management capabilities. Multiples for privately held investment management businesses are inexpensive. Virtus has a strong platform and history of integrating acquisitions to realize synergies and create value.
  5. Consistent Dividends and Stock Repurchases – VRTS management has been consistent in returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases. VRTS stock currently yields 5.6%. Stock repurchases further enhance shareholder value by reducing outstanding shares and increasing earnings per share (EPS). In Q1, VRTS management repurchased about 2% of the stock and signaled that they would be more aggressive given the stock price decline from early March. We would encourage management to consider increasing leverage by 1.0x EBITDA and repurchase 30% of the shares.

There are risks to our investment thesis on Virtus:

  1. Challenged Flows Due to Active Management Out of Favor - Despite its strengths, Virtus Investment Partners faces challenges in asset flows, primarily due to the growing trend towards passive management. Active management has fallen out of favor with some investors, who prefer the lower fees and perceived simplicity of passive strategies. This shift poses a headwind for Virtus, potentially impacting on its ability to attract and retain assets. The firm must navigate this evolving landscape by demonstrating the value of active management and differentiating its offerings through superior performance and client service.
  2. Some Past Acquisitions May Not Have Paid Off - Virtus's acquisition strategy, while generally successful, has not been without its missteps. Some past acquisitions have not delivered the anticipated returns. These underperforming acquisitions can weigh on financial results and erode investor confidence.
  3. Waiting for Flows to Improve or Management to Take Action - Virtus Investment Partners is at a crossroads, awaiting improvement in asset flows or action from management to make an acquisition or materially increase share repurchases. This state of waiting creates uncertainty and requires patience from investors.

In summary, Virtus Investment Partners presents a compelling investment case with a robust business model, attractive valuation, low debt levels, and a clear commitment to shareholder returns. However, the firm must navigate challenges related to asset flows and past acquisitions. Investors should weigh these strengths and weaknesses carefully, considering Virtus's potential for growth and the strategic actions needed to address its current hurdles. With prudent management and strategic execution, Virtus Investment Partners could deliver substantial value to its shareholders over the long term.

data.title: TransDigm: Strong Profitability Amid Mixed Results, But Valuation Still Stretched
data.description:
Introduction

TransDigm (NYSE:TDG) has been a steady performer for years as a serial acquirer in the spare parts business of selling to government and commercial customers in the aircraft aftermarket. As the company just reported its latest earnings last week, this article will be an update to my previous coverage on the company.

Recent Results

When looking at the latest Q2’25 results for TransDigm, the company reported mixed results with a miss on the revenue figure but a beat on the EPS number. On revenues, sales came in at $2.15 billion, good for an 11% year over year increase but a miss of $17.7 million compared to sellside estimates. About 7% of this growth was organic revenue contribution.

By segment, TransDigm’s commercial aftermarket led the charge growing 13% as all submarkets including business jet freight and interior outperformed the segment average while passenger-related revenues particularly those tied to engine content showed solid gains. Defense revenues rose 9% with balanced growth across OEM and aftermarket channels supported by strong bookings that outpaced sales. Commercial OEM revenues however remained flat compared to last year as the segment was impacted by persistent supply chain and labor challenges though sequential growth of 17% from Q1’25 signaled improving production trends particularly for Boeing’s 737 MAX. I believe this mix of market dynamics underscores TransDigm’s strength in high-margin aftermarket segments which continue to drive profitability.

Speaking of profitability, I’d view TransDigm’s profitability metrics as the highlight of the quarter. On EBITDA, the company’s $1.19 billion in EBITDA came to a 54% margin as a favorable mix of commercial aftermarket sales led to margin expansion of 80bps. As a result of this aftermarket contribution growth, even with a higher GAAP tax rate, the company’s EPS came in at $9.11, representing a 14% increase and a 16 beat versus Wall Street estimates.

In terms of some of the key highlights from the quarter, CEO Kevin Stein’ announced his retirement and he will remain with the company until the end of FY’25. Replacing him will be Co-COO Mike Lisman set to assume the role in October. While Kevin Stein was at the helm for a while, I think it’s good to see TransDigm hiring from within – a testament to the fact that they have talent inside the company to move people up to executive positions.

While M&A was pretty soft this quarter, the company explained why they’re taking a disciplined approach to M&A, as shown by the company not pursuing Boeing’s Jeppesen business. According to management, while the M&A pipeline remains active with a focus on small to midsize targets that fit the proprietary high-aftermarket model, the elevated multiples in the aerospace sector have posed challenges, particularly for larger deals. Overall, I think it makes sense to slow down on M&A if you can’t find targets at the right price and buy back stock instead.

Given the ample free cash flow generation, TransDigm’s balance sheet is solid. With $2.4 billion in cash, the company’s liquidity is strong and while net debt is high at $12.8 billion, the company’s net debt to EBITDA ratio of 5.1x is down 0.2 turns from the prior quarter. I think a lot of investors would look at that leverage and conclude that TransDigm’s leverage is unsustainable and risky. However, given that the company has exhibited strong pricing power, limited competition, and a predictable replacement cycle in the aftermarket parts business specifically, I think it’s reasonable to remain at above average levels of leverage.

After all, leverage is likely to continue on its trajectory lower while valuations are elevated (until TransDigm finds more targets to acquire) and about three quarters of the company’s $25 billion in debt is hedged over the next two years through derivatives. In terms of return of capital to shareholders, TransDigm has upped its share repurchases this quarter buying back 40,000 shares for $50 million at an average price of $1250 per share followed by an additional 100,000 shares for $130 million in early April. This tells me management is confident in the intrinsic value of its stock viewing these repurchases as accretive investments akin to M&A. In the valuation section of this article, I’ll explain why I think TransDigm’s multiple seems fairly priced here (compared to being historically overvalued).

Outlook

TransDigm reaffirmed their guidance for FY’25 with no changes. As shown below, their guidance reflects somewhat of a slow recovery in the macro environment and ongoing aerospace market recovery. Full year revenues are expected to be around $8.85 billion (an 11% increase over 2024) with EBITDA expected at $4.685 billion, implying a 52.9% margin. Adjusted earnings per share is guided to be at $36.47, up 7% year over year.

I believe this guidance, unchanged despite a strong Q2, embeds a degree of caution (particularly given the potential for lumpiness in margins and the evolving tariff landscape). The commercial aftermarket which is poised to grow in the high single-digit to low double-digit range should be additive to TransDigm’s organic growth drivers supported by strong bookings and point-of-sale data through distribution channels. Defense revenues are now projected by management to grow in the high single-digit to low double-digit range while commercial OEM growth is for slightly to the low to mid single-digit range due to softer business jet and helicopter submarkets. In my view, this outlook suggests steady progress in the short-term and I think TransDigm is in a good spot to capitalize on aftermarket demand and improving OEM production rates like for Boeing’s 737 MAX.

In the short term, I expect the next couple of quarters to reflect continued strength in the commercial aftermarket and defense markets, with commercial OEM revenues gradually improving as supply chain bottlenecks ease. It’s possible that margins fluctuate here due to the anticipated uptick in lower-margin OEM sales, but I believe TransDigm’s operational discipline (especially around capital allocation and SG&A) will keep profitability within a narrow band around the guided 52.9% for the year.

Looking longer term, I think the outlook looks rather compelling for TransDigm. The company’s got a proprietary product portfolio and high-margin aftermarket exposure, which provide resilience across aerospace cycles. With a disciplined capital allocation strategy that prioritizes organic investment, accretive M&A, and shareholder returns, I think investors could do quite well (barring valuation risk). With an expanding M&A pipeline, we’ll have to see how valuations in the space fare but even without deals to chew on in the near-term, there’s still room to buyback stock and pay down debt. Historically, TransDigm has been able to grow their EPS at double digits and I see no reason why that’s about to change anytime soon.

Risks

There’s risk to any investment and TransDigm isn’t without exception. In TransDigm’s case, I think the biggest risk I see would be a potential slowdown in commercial aircraft demand. Given that the segment accounts for a sizable portion of the company’s revenues (and an even larger percentage of earnings), this would be a negative for TransDigm.

Recent announcements from U.S. airlines about capacity reductions and curtailed maintenance spending raise a few concerns about softer demand for aftermarket parts. So although TransDigm’s Q2’25 bookings and point-of-sale data remained strong into April, I think a broader economic softening or prolonged airline schedule cuts could pressure the high single-digit to low double-digit growth guidance for this segment. In my view, this risk is heightened by the tight correlation between aftermarket demand and metrics like takeoffs and landings and a sustained downturn in aftermarket activity would almost certainly compress margins and earnings.

Another risk TransDigm faces is with its M&A strategy. As a cornerstone of its capital allocation strategy, TransDigm has benefitted significantly from using leverage to finance its M&A activities and while that’s been highly successful in a low rate environment (not to mention the company’s track record of integrating those deals), I think leverage looks high at the moment and it will take several quarters for that leverage to come down as the company prioritizes debt reduction. With rates where they are and markets only expecting that the Fed will cut rates from their current level of 4.25% to 4.5% by December, probably to under 4%, that’s a lot lower than what the market was expecting at the start of the year. If TransDigm’s interest rates start to go up on its debt as maturities come time to refinance, interest expenses could take a bite out of the company’s free cash flow.

Lastly, on tariffs, I think TransDigm’s business isn’t going to be that affected by trade disruption that we’re seeing in other industries. For one thing, TransDigm’s business is predominantly domestic manufacturing based and the company implemented cost-saving measures in advance of the tariffs to mitigate potential headwinds. As well, commercial aftermarket bookings significantly outpaced sales and point-of-sale data through distribution partners grew at double-digit rates which should be an indicator of sustained demand going forward.

Valuation

On valuation, TransDigm isn’t cheap – and this is one of the main sticking points to why I haven’t bought the stock. When looking at the company’s multiples, the company trades for 20.5x EV/EBITDA and 34.6x P/E, which are well ahead of public comparables that as a group trade for 13.6x EV/EBITDA and 28.6x P/E (source: Bloomberg). Given the long-term track record of TransDigm, it’s ability to integrate M&A successfully, and the organic growth profile of its core business, I think that the company deserves a slight premium valuation, however, I think the gap seems too wide for now.

This valuation is also ahead of the company’s own historical average valuation. As illustrated in the chart below, the stock sits at the higher end of its ten-year historical average EV/EBITDA range.

Finally, most analysts are pretty bullish on TransDigm. Of the 23 analysts who cover the stock, there are 18 ‘buy’ ratings, and 5 ‘hold’ ratings. With an average price target of $1521.32, the stock is currently trading close to analysts’ price targets one year out, suggesting that the stock might be close to fully valued .

Conclusion

I’m of the view that TransDigm is still a high-quality aerospace company with a resilient business model in the high-margin commercial aftermarket. Despite a slight revenue miss in Q2’25, strong profitability through improving metrics and growing free cash flow signal to me that the company’s operating well. So far, I think TransDigm has shown a good capital allocation through disciplined M&A (pulling back when valuations are expensive or when leverage is high) and opportunistic buybacks. While risks such as elevated leverage, rising interest rates, and potential softness in commercial aircraft demand warrant caution, the company’s long-term fundamentals remain compelling. Given its solid financials, durable competitive advantages, and improving OEM trends, TransDigm appears well-positioned to deliver steady earnings growth. That said, with the stock trading near the upper end of its historical valuation range, near 52-week highs, and close to consensus price targets, I think new investors may want to wait for a better entry point before initiating a position.

data.title: Cintas: More Manufacturing Equals More Growth; But Overvalued
data.description:
Cintas (NASDAQ:CTAS) is a leading player in the uniform rental and facility services markets, serving over one million businesses across a broad range of industries. I favor the company’s leading position in the uniform rental market, potentially benefiting from the manufacturing expansion in the US market. However, I think the stock price is overvalued and I am initiating with a ‘Sell’ rating with a fair value of $176 per share.

As depicted in chart below, Cintas has delivered strong organic revenue growth, averaging above 10% over the past three years.

The strong organic growth was primarily driven by several factors:

  • Uniform Rental and Facility Services represent over 77% of total revenue, and the segment has been growing at a high-single-digit rate in recent years, driven by both volume and pricing growth. As a leader, Cintas has strong pricing power over its customers. As discussed in its Q3 FY25 earnings call, pricing has contributed around 2% to organic revenue growth in recent quarters. I anticipate the company will continue to pass on any inflationary costs to their end-customers, making a growth driver for their business.
  • Cintas has a high retention rate in its uniform rental and facility services, as highlighted in the call. The company believes their one-stop shopping experience and superior client services support their high retention rate, which I believe are crucial for revenue growth and margin expansion.
  • As the largest player in the US market, Cintas enjoys the benefits from high routs densities. As the company is servicing a huge number of customers, the service person can improve their productivities due to the route densities. Compared to small players, Cintas can easily deliver higher margin with better service quality to their customers.

Cintas tried to acquire UniFirst (UNF) but terminated the deal due to unsuccessful negotiations in March 2025. I think the termination benefits Cintas’s shareholders for the following reasons.

It was a quite large deal as Cintas proposed to acquire of the outstanding common and class B shares of UniFirst for $275.00 per share. I generally think large deal transactions are less likely to be successful. I understand that acquisition could enable Cintas to increase its market share in uniform rental market in the US market; however, the deal integration could present a big challenge for Cintas.

In addition, UniFirst is a not a well-managed company with sluggish growth, as illustrated in the chart below:

As such, I consider the termination of the acquisition a positive event for Cintas’s shareholder.

Cintas delivered its Q3 FY25 result on March 26th, posting 7.9% organic revenue growth and 17.1% operating income growth, as detailed in the table below. It is impressive to deliver high-single-digit revenue growth in the current economic environment, in my view.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the potential benefits from manufacturing expansion in the US market. With the Trump administration aiming to use tariffs as a weapon to encourage the return of manufacturing jobs to the US, Cintas’s uniform rental and services could potentially benefit from this long-term trend. If the manufacturing jobs increase over time, Cintas’s revenue growth could potentially accelerate in the near future, in my opinion.

Cintas is guiding for 7.7% organic revenue growth for FY25, as shown in the table below. As the company has delivered three quarters of results, I don’t anticipate the full-year result will deviate from its guidance. As such, I assume the company will deliver 7.4% total revenue growth in FY25, aligned with its guidance.

From FY26 onwards, I anticipate the company will deliver 7.5% organic revenue growth, assuming 2% pricing growth and 5.5% volume growth, aligned with the company’s long-term historical growth averages. The volume growth reflects 4% industrial market growth and 1.5% market share gains. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 2% of revenue towards M&A, adding 100bps to the topline growth.

I model 30bsp annual margin expansion, driven by 10bps from pricing growth and 20bps from reduction in SG&A. I calculate the total operating expenses will grow by 8% annually and the operating margin is anticipated to reach 24.6% by FY34.

DCF summary:

I calculate the free cash flow from firm (FCFF) as follows:

I calculate the WACC to be 8.6% assuming: risk free 4.2%; beta 0.75; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $87 billion; debt $2.5 billion; tax rate 20%. I assume a 5% terminal growth rate incorporating 2% pricing growth and 3% terminal volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $176 per share.

Upside Risks

As I assign a ‘Sell’ rating, I am considering the following upside risks:

  • As discussed previously, if Trump’s policies are effective, more manufacturing jobs could be created in the US market. In that case, Cintas’s revenue is more likely to accelerate in the future. If I change my organic revenue growth to 9% from FY26 onwards, Cintas’s stock price will be fairly valued, as per my DCF. Furthermore, the market tends to favor those companies with accelerating growth potential.
  • Due to the termination of UniFirst acquisition, Cintas has a strong balance sheet that could allow for increased dividend payments and share repurchases. Although the company did not repurchase any shares in Q3 FY25, they could potentially buyback their shares in the future, which could provide some support for its stock price.
  • Cintas is a US-centric business without international operations. The company could potentially expand into foreign markets via acquisitions to broaden its market reach and increase its total addressable markets.

End Notes

Due to its valuation, I stay on the sidelines and wait for a better entry point. I am initiating with a ‘Sell’ rating on Cintas with a fair value of $176 per share.

data.title: The RealReal: Impressive Buyer Growth, But We're Waiting On Margin Progress
data.description:
When tariffs were first announced in April, nearly all news headlines pointed to the very near certainty of a U.S. recession: even now, when tariffs are at bay, this may not be an outcome we're able to avoid. With so many consumer-facing companies sounding off cautious notes and declining same-store/same-restaurant sales growth, we're rightly impressed when a consumer stock is able to post strong sales.

Even before tariff volatility was introduced, The RealReal (NASDAQ:REAL) was already undergoing a challenging business transition, lopping off its more unpredictable direct-sales model to move toward a consignment-first approach. Clearly, this rebrand as well as The RealReal's re-commitment to high luxury brands only has worked, as reflected in the company's return to double-digit revenue growth and new buyer additions. And yet, the stock market continues to be cautious on The RealReal, with its stock down ~40% year to date and slumping further after its Q1 earnings release.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on The RealReal in February, when the stock was trading closer to $7 per share. Since then, The RealReal has seen its share price decay further, while the company continues to see growth in GMV, active buyers, and revenue. I'm moving directionally more positive on the company, but need to see more concrete evidence of a path to improved EBITDA margins before I buy into this stock, at least at current share prices. For now, I remain neutral on the name.

In a nutshell, here are the factors that have moved incrementally positive on The RealReal:

  • The RealReal seems to have found its "sweet spot" in merchandise assortment that is allowing the company to aggressively grow its buyer base. In Q1, the company listed its best selling "obsession trends" as branded jewelry (BVLGARI, Cartier) and mid-tier handbags (Balenciaga, Loewe). A healthy supply pool of these top-selling products is helping the company to grow and retain its active buyer base, validating its shift to a consignment model.
  • GMV growth in a tough environment. Many retail companies have reported shrinking sales, and e-commerce platforms like Etsy (ETSY) and Wayfair (W) have seen declining GMVs. The RealReal has bucked the trend, with its affluent customer segment apparently more immune to macro shocks.

At the same time, however, I remain concerned on the company's currently razor-thin profit margins. The RealReal has spent heavily on operations in order to move to the consignment model and to introduce new features such as its AI-assisted listing tool, Athena, which the company notes is cutting down processing times for listing new items by ~20%. We need to see evidence that The RealReal's return to growth is yielding economies of scale to a point that helps to justify the stock's valuation: because at present, the company sits at rich multiples of EBITDA.

At current share prices in the mid-$5s, The RealReal trades at a market cap of $639.6 million. After we net off the $139.6 million of cash against the company's rather meaty $398.9 million debt pile (or a $259.6 million net debt position, which is a substantial multiple of its earnings power), the company's resulting enterprise value is $898.9 million.

Meanwhile, for FY25, The RealReal has maintained its full-year outlook (which I view as an accomplishment in a Q1 earnings season in which many consumer-facing companies have cut their guidance instead) at $645-$660 million in revenue (7-10% y/y growth) and $20-$30 million in adjusted EBITDA, or a 3.8% margin at the midpoint (+220bps y/y).

This puts The RealReal's valuation at 36x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. In other words, for the company to grow into its valuation at a more normal low/mid-teens adjusted EBITDA multiple, we'd need to see adjusted EBITDA margins roughly triple to ~10% from current levels, on top of stable high single digit/low teens revenue growth.

Now, all indicators from recent results showcase that The RealReal is poised to get there. But at the same time, I don't see an immediate rush to buy this stock ahead of any meaningful catalysts (like layoffs, which the company hasn't done since 2023) or increased contribution from tertiary revenue streams like seller services that make me want to buy this stock today.

All in all, I think The RealReal is moving in the right direction: but I'd prefer to monitor this stock from the sidelines for now until we see evidence of the fundamental winds changing.

Q1 download

Let's now dig through The RealReal's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

The RealReal's revenue grew 11% y/y to $160.0 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $159.8 million (also 11% y/y) while impressively accelerating two points versus 9% y/y growth in Q4.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases that the company's GMV (gross merchandise value, the sum total value of the goods that transacts on The RealReal's platform) has also increased 9% y/y to $490 million. Again, while The RealReal occupies a relatively small niche in the retail space, it is an outlier among e-commerce platforms in reporting high single digit growth in GMV. Wayfair's revenue fell -1% y/y in the same quarter (since it's a first party platform, its GMV and revenue are one and the same), while Etsy's GMV fell -6.5% y/y. Clearly here, The RealReal's orientation toward higher-income consumers is also showcasing less elasticity toward toughening macro conditions. The company's count of active buyers also rose by 13k sequentially to 985k buyers, up 7% y/y.

It's important to recognize that The RealReal has also waded back into direct sales through a "reimagined" service that it calls Get Paid Now, in which The RealReal purchases inventory directly from consignors. The company leverages its data to determine which pieces are safe buys, generally higher-end products (the company notes that inventory sold through Get Paid Now has a 10x greater ASP than consigned items) and insists that this business is more profitable than it was in the prior iteration. Per CEO Rati Levesque's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Get Paid Now is an offering for consignors on the select list of high-end in-demand brands in just three categories. Watches, handbags and fine jewelry. Through Get Paid Now, we purchase inventory and these sales flow into our direct revenue, which to be clear is an entirely different and significantly more profitable business than it was a year ago.

Today, we've reimagined the direct business, and this revenue is driven by high-value supply from consignors and select vendors. Average selling prices for this merchandise we acquired through Get Paid Now is more than 10 times higher than our average selling price."

Direct revenue grew 61% y/y to $20.5 million, representing 13% of sales. On the Q&A portion of the earnings call, CFO Ajay Gopal stressed the fact that Get Paid Now carries a 25.5% gross margin, from 3% previously. It's clear that restarting direct sales is a meaningful growth driver in acquiring high-end supply for the company, but it's also a lower-margin activity than consignment and comes with inventory risk that investors should monitor carefully.

We note that the stock market was still roaring in Q1, and underwent significant volatility in Q2. We may see buying patterns for affluent customers shift in Q2 and the back half of this year if they decide to restrain discretionary spending on account of gyrating portfolio balances.

On the profitability front, The RealReal posted an adjusted EBITDA of $4.1 million, at a 2.6% margin that improved 420bps y/y.

The pace of margin expansion is impressive: yet we do note that the company's earnings power is still very limited, especially in the context of its huge debt balance. The RealReal's ~$260 million of net debt stands at a >10x multiple of its midpoint $25 million adjusted EBITDA guidance for this year (conservative commercial banks typically set 3x as the threshold for a highly leveraged company).

This underscores my main thesis and concern on The RealReal: while supply-chasing initiatives and a reinstatement of direct sales have helped The RealReal achieve aggressive buyer and revenue growth, we're still waiting for concrete evidence that this top-line strength will eventually flow through in terms of the more meaningful profitability that is needed to support both the company's valuation and its debt load.

Key takeaways

To me, The RealReal is still a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the one hand, we appreciate the company's recent momentum and its ability to monetize both consignment and direct sales. At the same time, however, we're concerned about The RealReal's ability to bolster its margin profile to meaningfully higher levels needed to justify the company's valuation.

Watch this name from the sidelines and don't rush in to buy.

data.title: Kinross Gold: Tracking Well Against Annual Guidance
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. Costs = production cost of sales, and they are not comparable to AISC reported by most producers on a mine-site basis.

Q1 Production & Sales

Kinross Gold (NYSE:KGC)(TSX:K:CA) released its Q1-25 results last week, reporting quarterly production of ~512,000 gold-equivalent ounces, a 3% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to lower grades at c (Mauritania), lower grades and recoveries at La Coipa (Chile), and lower grades and throughput at Round Mountain (Nevada), partially offset by higher grades at Fort Knox (Alaska) and Paracatu (Brazil). On a positive note, Kinross is still in a solid position to meet its 2025 guidance of ~2.0 million GEOs, with output sitting at just over 25.6% of full-year expectations.

As for Kinross' quarterly production by jurisdiction, it has improved since last decade (Q1-25: ~34%) with a heavy weighting previously coming from Russia (Kupol-Dvoinoye) and Ghana (Chirano) combined and with it having less than ~30% of production coming from Tier-1 operations for most of 2020/2021. That said, Kinross still lags its major producer peers with closer to 35% of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (defined as Canada, the United States, Australia, Finland, and Sweden) vs. peers like Agnico Eagle (AEM), Alamos Gold (AGI) and Northern Star Resources (OTCPK:NESRF) which have between 80% to 100% of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, while the bulk of Kinross production comes from Brazil and Mauritania.

Tasiast

Digging into the operations a little closer, Tasiast produced ~137,600 ounces in Q1-25 at $811/oz unit costs (production cost of sales), down from ~159,200 ounces at $660/oz in the year-ago period. Lower production was related to a dip in throughput (~1.93 million tonnes vs. ~2.07 million tonnes) at lower grades (2.15 G/T of gold vs. 2.46 G/T of gold), offset by a significant improvement in recoveries to 94.7%. The company noted that lower grades were related to blending West Branch 4 ore with stockpiles, with mining shifting from West Branch 4 to capital development at West Branch 5. On recoveries, this was driven by optimization initiatives at the mill, helping to offset the lower grade profile.

Unfortunately, Kinross reported another mill fire at Tasiast in mid-April (since back online) with roughly three weeks of downtime, which will contribute to a weaker Q2. The good news is that the company is sticking to its annual guidance of 500,000 ounces. However, investors should prepare for higher mine-site AISC over the remainder of the year with higher royalties (gold price), and lower production on average relative to Q1-25 and just ~13% of planned annual sustaining capital spent in Q1-25 (~$13.7 million vs. ~$105 million).

Paracatu

Moving to its Paracatu Mine, production came in at ~146,600 ounces at costs of $951/oz, an improvement from ~128,300 ounces at $1,059/oz. Higher production was related to a better grade profile year-over-year with ~12.5 million tonnes processed at 0.43 G/T of gold at better recoveries (82.5% vs. 79.0%), up from grades of 0.31 G/T of gold in Q1-24, albeit partially offset by lower throughput (harder ore). Kinross noted recoveries benefited from additional gravity circuit infrastructure and Paracatu is tracking well against guidance with ~25% of planned output delivered in Q1-25.

That said, and similar to Tasiast, mine-site AISC will increase materially in the coming quarters, with just ~$24.4 million of a planned ~$195 million spent on sustaining capital in the period (~12.5%). This suggests significant catch-up in coming quarters and when combined with the stronger gold price, we should see quite a bit of pressure on AISC given the back-end weighed sustaining capital profile for its two largest operations.

La Coipa

As for Kinross' La Coipa Mine, production came in at ~52,300 ounces at $1,147/oz costs, down from ~71,300 ounces at $733/oz in the year-ago period. This represented a significant dip in production at much higher costs, impacted by lower silver grades (~32 G/T of silver vs. ~87 G/T of silver), and lower gold recoveries which more than offset slightly higher gold grades (2.19 G/T of gold vs. 2.09 G/T of gold). This was a significant drag on costs in the quarter given that this was one of the lowest-cost operations in the year-ago period, pushing Kinross' company-wide cost of sales per GEO to $1,038/oz (Q1-24: $982/oz) when combined with higher costs at Tasiast.

Fort Knox

Looking at Fort Knox, production came in at ~112,100 GEOs (100% basis) or ~94,300 on an attributable basis, up from ~53,400 GEOs in the year-ago period. This was related to contribution from significantly higher-grade ore trucked from Manh Choh (70%), with ~294,000 tonnes processed from Manh Choh in the quarter. Given the significantly improved grade profile for mill feed with Manh Choh ore (2.77 G/T of gold vs. 0.67 G/T of gold), recoveries also ticked higher (90.8% vs. 76.4%) and unit costs improved to $1,176/oz vs. $1,466/oz.

Nevada

Finally, looking at its Nevada operations, Round Mountain and Bald Mountain combined for production of ~81,200 ounces, a sharp decline from ~115,400 ounces in the year-ago period. At Round Mountain, lower grades contributed to a 47% dip in output at higher costs ($1,585/oz vs. $1,329/oz), with this related to 2025 being a transitional year with the completion of mining at Phase W to stripping at Phase S, with ore to be available in H2-25. As for Bald Mountain, Kinross shared that Redbird (new pit) mining is schedule, and the company is progressing detailed engineering for a potential Phase 2 extension of Redbird which is certainly supported by higher gold prices.

If approved, this could smooth out its long-term production profile by adding another ~700,000 ounces to the mine plan and provide time for Great Bear to move into commercial production, which is likely closer to a Q4 2030 event.

Financial Results

Moving to Kinross' financial results, quarterly revenue came in at ~$1,498 million (+38% year-over-year), while operating cash flow soared to ~$597 million (+59% year-over-year), and attributable free cash flow improved to ~$371 million (+155% year-over-year). These metrics certainly benefited from Kinross' record average realized gold price of $2,857/oz (Q1-24: $2,070/oz) partially offset by fewer ounces sold, while free cash flow benefited from lower year-over-year capex (~$204 million vs. ~$232 million).

Overall, this allowed Kinross to fully repay its term loan ($200 million paid in the quarter), end the quarter with ~$695 million in cash, and improve its net debt position to ~$540 million. That said, Kinross is tracking well behind planned capex of $1.15 billion (~18% of annual guidance) and has a higher cost profile as the year progresses. So, while the gold price is certainly a nice tailwind currently and Q1-25 free cash flow was impacted by annual tax payments in Brazil/Mauritania, we will see some offset from higher-cost producer and catch up on planned capex (implied average quarterly capex of $300+ million even assuming just $1.12 billion in total capex vs. guide of $1.15 billion).

Costs & Margins

Moving to costs and margins, Kinross certainly beat some of its major peers from an AISC standpoint in the quarter, reporting quarterly all-in sustaining costs of $1,355/oz, just a 3% increase vs. the year-ago period. This contributed to record AISC margins of $1,502/oz (52.6%) vs. $760/oz (36.7%) in the year-ago period due to the record gold price.

However, investors should not expect similar results in coming quarters. In fact, Kinross is guiding for $1,500/oz AISC and that is at a $2,500/oz gold price assumption, with a $5/oz impact per $100/oz move in the gold price. Further, sustaining capital is sitting at just ~17% of annual guidance, with Q1-25 AISC benefiting from much lower sustaining capital spend (~$92 million vs. ~$109 million in Q1-24).

Given this setup, I would expect to see significant cost increases as the year progresses, with full-year AISC likely to land closer to $1,510/oz and more in line with guidance.

Obviously, this is hardly an issue, and Kinross' AISC is still well below the industry average. However, investors should be cautioned about simply dragging right on costs and modeling Q1-25, given that actual costs are likely to creep up towards the end of the year and take another leg higher to $1,600/oz plus in 2026 with the impact of lower production at its two lower-cost operations, Tasiast and La Coipa, in addition to what could be a more competitive labor environment at these gold prices.

Recent Developments

Great Bear

As for the company's Great Bear Project in Canada, drilling has moved to working to identify regional opportunities on the large ~120 square kilometer land package, with over 50,000 meters planned to be drilled in 2025. As for its Advanced Exploration program [AEX], the company noted that construction and earthworks are underway and detailed engineering was near complete. For those unfamiliar, AEX is a much smaller project vs. the main project with plans under AEX being to extract a ~60,000 tonne bulk sample, with plans to construct two surface portals and ramps which will later be used for hauling ore once the project is fully constructed.

As for the Main Project, Kinross noted that it has begun detailed engineering on the mill and key site infrastructure and that procurement for major process equipment will be initiated in late 2025. Once in production, the asset will be a game-changer for Kinross with the potential for ~430,000 ounces per annum at ~$900/oz AISC (inflation and gold price adjusted) over the life of mine, with production in the first 8 years closer to 520,000 ounces at lower unit costs, making this one of the lowest-cost mines globally next to Alamos' Island Gold Complex.

Phase X Drilling

While Great Bear has been the highlight for exploration in the portfolio, Kinross delivered solid results from Phase X at its Round Mountain Project, with impressive hits of ~98 meters at 4.2 G/T of gold, ~56 meters at 5.1 G/T of gold, ~53 meters at 4.1 G/T of gold and ~101 meters at 3.8 G/T of gold. These are some of the better intercepts being drilled across Nevada and follow an exceptional intercept that was previously reported of ~32.4 meters at 29.6 G/T of gold, with Kinross sharing in its Q1-25 Conference Call that is remains confident in a 3-4 G/T gold deposit at Phase X, helping to spike grades relative to lower grades at Round Mountain OP.

As for progress on this future mid-grade bulk underground mine, Kinross shared that ~3,900 meters of decline development has been completed to date. Investors should get a better idea of the potential of this asset later this year, with Kinross hoping to complete an underground resource estimate and project update here by year-end.

Share Buyback

Given Kinross' improved free cash flow profile ($1.4+ billion likely in 2025), the company noted that it has reactivated its share buyback program, with ~4.3 million shares repurchased after quarter-end. While this will certainly help to reduce the company's share count and provide an incremental return to shareholders, the buyback program that allows for up to 10% of its share count (~110 million shares) with a "minimum target of $500 million" (assuming gold prices are sustained and operations deliver on plan) is being reactivated at much less favorable levels.

Obviously, everyone has their own views on share buybacks and timing, but I far prefer them to be done opportunistically when they have more impact, like we've seen from sector leaders such as Alamos Gold. In Kinross' case, it would be retiring shares at much higher levels when valuations are much less attractive if it goes ahead with this share buyback program, and the below chart highlights this, with ~$400 million combined in shares repurchased (2021/2023) when the stock hung out in the US$3.00 - US$6.00 range, and now plans to purchase at least 25% more per its minimum target when the share price has tripled.

Under Kinross' previous buyback, I thought the move was brilliant, retiring shares at depressed levels at a very attractive valuation near what looked to be a cyclical bottom for the sector and commodity. However, we can see that Kinross' valuation is now back towards the upper portion of its long-term range. Obviously, this doesn't mean that the stock can't go higher, and maybe these purchases will work out short-term to medium-term. However, I simply prefer companies that repurchase shares in a more disciplined manner and buying more shares at 3x higher prices at a lofty valuation is not that, especially when the minimum target is not being used opportunistically.

Valuation

Based on ~1,236 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$13.80, Kinross trades at a market cap of ~$17.1 billion and an enterprise value of ~$17.6 billion. This leaves Kinross trading near the middle of the pack in terms of its multiple and at roughly ~12x FY2025 EV/FCF estimates assuming a $3,150/oz gold price (YTD price: ~$2,990/oz). And while this may suggest further upside, Kinross is now more expensive than larger and more diversified peers and is also back to trading in the upper range of its historical price to cash flow range.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 8.0x FY2025 cash flow estimates and 1.1x P/NAV (7%), I see a fair value for KGC of US$13.90. This suggests that the stock remains fully valued at current levels. Obviously, a rising tide in the form of the gold price will lift all boats and the stock could trade higher if gold marches to new highs or re-tests its highs at $3,500/oz. That said, I don't see any margin of safety at current levels, and I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector.

Summary

Kinross had a solid Q1-25 report with ~512,000 GEOs produced at $1,355/oz AISC, but costs should creep up in coming quarters with catch up on sustaining capital and I would not be surprised to see AISC head above $1,600/oz in 2026, with Q1-25 marking trough costs on a medium-term basis. This is hardly a big deal with the gold price strengthening quarter-over-quarter, but with elevated bullish sentiment for the stock, limited margin of safety and a rising cost trend, I see a high risk to starting new positions at current levels, especially if the gold price were to falter.

To summarize, I don't see any way to justify chasing KGC above US$14.00, and I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere today.

data.title: Tariffs Are Compromising Trump's Economic Agenda
data.description:
By Philip Cross

President’s Trump "Liberation Day" announcement of higher tariffs was supposed to trigger massive reshoring and restructuring of the US economy towards its manufacturing industries.

Instead, it provoked a sharp drop in financial markets. Such a widespread drop typically signals the onset of recession, not the redistribution of labor and capital from sectors that are damaged by tariffs to the few that may prosper. The potential for recession is evident in the widespread sell-off in US stock markets, slumping commodity prices, a sharp devaluation of the US exchange rate, and lower short-term interest rates. The anomaly of rising bond yields in a weak economy reflects the increased menace of stagflation, as higher tariffs fuel higher expectations for inflation even as recession fears pummel stocks.

The retreat in financial market prices highlights the false dichotomy between the interests of Wall Street and Main Street cited by many pundits. For example, the latest reading on small business sentiment about the direction of the economy from the National Federation of Independent Business saw a 16-point drop to its lowest level since 2020. Main Street cannot thrive when investors shift funds to the bond market or to other countries and when the wealth of Americans is shrinking rapidly. The outlook is grim even for manufacturers, based on surveys by the Federal Reserve branches in New York and Philadelphia. Put simply, Trump’s tariffs are already plainly hurting those whom they were apparently meant to help.

Larger companies, whose market valuation has fallen even more than small firms, have the additional problem of dealing with the international damage to the US image as a reliable partner. Boycotts of American brands already are inflicting significant damage on the US economy that cannot be negotiated in treaties between governments and likely will persist long after higher tariffs are repealed; for example, Canadian visitors - the largest source of international tourism to the US - plunged 17 percent in March. Canada is not alone; an Ifop poll in France found 62 percent support a boycott of American companies.

The shift of trillions of dollars from private sector equities to public sector bonds is the exact reverse of what is needed to sustain America’s prosperity. The slump in the stock market and commodity prices, especially those most closely linked to industrial demand such as copper, shows investors know that the damage from tariffs will be much greater than the increase in investment the Trump administration cites as the justification for tariffs. The devaluation of the US dollar testifies to investors avoiding its assets, the opposite of Mr. Trump’s claim that “Money is pouring in and we want to keep it that way.” Markets have rallied in recent days in lockstep with administration actions to roll back tariffs and expectations that agreements with other countries will avert full-scale trade wars.

The one benefit of the carnage in financial markets is it should put to rest the wonky belief that tariffs are a boon to economic growth for at least a generation. The disastrous experience of the 1930s tariff war, which played a major role in the Great Depression and the Second World War, led to the creation of the post-war policy framework initiated by the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade in 1947 that lowered trade barriers steadily for over 60 years. Discrediting tariffs for decades to come is the one consolation to take from the current upheaval in global financial markets.

The tariff war already makes it unlikely that Mr. Trump will be able to duplicate the economic success of his first term. The positive effects of Trump’s lowering taxes and deregulation efforts will not be nearly as visible because of tariff-induced pain, and this will, in turn, damage the credibility of those of us who argue lower taxes and fewer regulations stimulate growth.

Even if tariffs are repealed and taxes and regulations are slashed later this year, investor confidence will not easily be restored by an administration that embraced such needlessly destructive and short-sighted policies despite ample warning of their dangers.
